metadata.title: Chapter 1: Introduction to Pharmacology
metadata.subtitle: Foundations of Nursing Pharmacology & Patient Safety
metadata.author: Atlas Delgado
metadata.textbookReference: Karch, A.M. (2020). Focus on Nursing Pharmacology (8th ed.). Wolters Kluwer.

chapterLevel.outcome: Define pharmacology and explain its relationship to nursing practice
Explain the role of nurses in medication safety and therapeutic outcomes
Trace the evolution of pharmacology from ancient to modern times
Classify drugs using multiple systems (therapeutic, chemical, regulatory)
Identify regulatory bodies (FDA, DEA, nursing boards) and their roles in drug safety
Distinguish between pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, and pharmacology
Recognize drug interactions and apply management strategies
Master three dosage calculation methods (formula, ratio-proportion, dimensional analysis)
Apply clinical judgment to medication safety decisions
Communicate effectively with prescribers about medication concerns

sections.title: Section 1: Overview & Introduction
learningObjectives: Understand why pharmacology is critical for nursing practice
Define pharmacology in the context of nursing
Recognize the nurse's role as the final line of defense in medication safety
Appreciate the historical evolution leading to modern pharmaceutical science
keyTakeaways: Pharmacology is clinical power — the difference between healing and harm
Nurses are the last line of defense between a medication order and a patient's body
Understanding pharmacology enables nurses to catch drug interactions, contraindications, and dosing errors
Pharmacology knowledge has evolved dramatically over the past 150+ years, from trial-and-error herbalism to precision medicine
Today's challenge: 10,000+ FDA-approved drugs, countless combinations, and maximum potential for harm if used carelessly

contentBlocks.title: The Critical Moment
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Picture this: You're on a busy medical-surgical floor. A patient is admitted with pneumonia. The hospitalist orders an antibiotic. The patient's chart flags an allergy. Your phone buzzes with a medication alert.

In the next 30 seconds, you will make a decision that affects whether this patient recovers safely or experiences a serious adverse event.

contentBlocks.title: Pharmacology Defined
contentBlocks.content: Pharmacology is the science that studies the effects of chemical substances (drugs) on living organisms and biological systems. It combines chemistry, physiology, pathophysiology, and clinical practice.
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Pharmacology

The science that studies the effects of chemical substances (drugs) on living organisms and biological systems.

contentBlocks.title: Mrs. Garcia: Drug Interaction Case
contentBlocks.description: Real-world example of how pharmacology knowledge prevents medical disasters
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Mrs. Garcia: A Real-World Drug Interaction

68-year-old admitted for heart failure on multiple medications: beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, diuretic, digoxin, warfarin. New order: NSAID for arthritis.

Without pharmacology knowledge: You might just give it.

With pharmacology knowledge: You recognize NSAIDs interact with her medications → potential acute kidney injury, digoxin toxicity, bleeding risk. You suggest alternatives and prevent a medical disaster.

What is a therapeutic drug action?

interactiveElements.prompt: You notice a medication order that seems unusual for your patient. What do you do?

sections.title: Section 1.1: Definition & Scope of Pharmacology
learningObjectives: Define core pharmacology terms
Understand five major branches of pharmacology
Apply nursing pharmacology principles to clinical decision-making
keyTakeaways: Pharmacokinetics = What the body does to the drug (ADME)
Pharmacodynamics = What the drug does to the body (mechanism of action)
Pharmacogenomics explains why drugs work differently in different people
Nursing pharmacology is APPLIED pharmacology — taking science and making it safe, effective, and patient-centered
Six key nursing responsibilities: Assessment, Clinical Judgment, Administration, Monitoring, Patient Education, Communication & Advocacy

contentBlocks.title: Core Terms

definitions.Drug: any chemical substance that affects biological function
definitions.Medicine: a drug used therapeutically to treat, prevent, or diagnose disease
definitions.Medication: the actual preparation given to a patient

contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Pharmacology
The science of drug-organism interactions
Drug
Any chemical substance that affects biological function
Medicine
A drug used therapeutically to treat, prevent, or diagnose disease
Medication
The actual preparation given to a patient

items.name: Pharmacokinetics (PK)
items.description: What the body does to the drug - Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Elimination (ADME)

items.name: Pharmacodynamics (PD)
items.description: What the drug does to the body - Mechanism of action, receptor binding, effects

items.name: Pharmacogenomics
items.description: Genetic variation in drug response - Why different patients need different doses

items.name: Toxicology
items.description: Study of harmful effects of drugs - Overdose, chronic toxicity, teratogenicity

items.name: Pharmacotherapy
items.description: Clinical application of pharmacology to treat disease - What nurses do daily

What are the three phases of drug metabolism?

sections.title: Section 1.2: Historical Context of Pharmacology
learningObjectives: Trace the evolution of pharmacology from ancient times to the modern era
Understand how historical events shaped current drug development and safety practices
Recognize the significance of major pharmaceutical breakthroughs
keyTakeaways: Ancient era: Trial-and-error herbalism; no understanding of mechanism
1897: Aspirin synthesized — first truly successful synthetic drug
1928: Penicillin discovered (Fleming, accidentally); changed medicine forever
1950s-60s: Antibiotic golden age; infectious diseases stopped being death sentences
1960s-present: Molecular biology reveals HOW drugs work; precision medicine emerging
Modern challenge: 10,000+ FDA-approved drugs; resistance and interactions as major concerns

events.keyMilestone: Willow bark, opium poppies, foxglove discovered empirically

events.era: Industrial Revolution: Isolation of Active Compounds
events.keyMilestone: Chemists begin extracting pure molecules from plants
Food and Drug Act passed; FDA established
Drug design based on understanding of receptors and mechanisms

contentBlocks.title: Era Comparison: What Changed
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
EraKey InsightNursing Implication
AncientPlants have effectsRespect that drugs are powerful
1800sChemistry reveals active ingredientsDrugs have specific mechanisms
1920s-40sAntibiotics save livesDrugs can cure diseases, not just palliate
1960s-70sMolecular targets identifiedUnderstand HOW drugs work
1980s-90sGenetics matterDifferent patients need different doses
2000s-presentPrecision medicineCombine genetics + pharmacology for individualized treatment

flashcardLinks: Define pharmacology
Trace the historical evolution of pharmacology

sections.title: Section 1.3: Drug Classification Systems
learningObjectives: Classify drugs using three major systems: therapeutic, chemical, and regulatory
Predict drug effects and side effects based on drug class
Understand DEA scheduling and its clinical implications
keyTakeaways: Therapeutic classification: Groups drugs by what disease they treat; helps predict side effects
Chemical classification: Explains drug properties (absorption route, interactions)
Regulatory (DEA) classification: Determines legal control and abuse potential (Schedule I-V)
Schedule II drugs (opioids, stimulants): High abuse potential; strict documentation required
Schedule IV drugs (benzodiazepines): Moderate abuse potential; habit-forming even at therapeutic doses
Recognizing drug name patterns (-olol, -pril, -sartan, -statin, -prazole) helps you immediately predict drug class and effects

contentBlocks.title: Classification System #1: By Therapeutic Use
contentBlocks.description: Grouping drugs by what disease/symptom they treat

patterns.predictableEffect: Lower heart rate and blood pressure
Similar to ACE inhibitors but no cough
Muscle pain possible; interact with grapefruit
Reduce stomach acid; long-term use risks
Risk of C. difficile; tendon rupture risk

schedules.description: No accepted medical use, high abuse potential
High abuse potential, some medical use
schedules.nursingRole: Strict documentation; monitor for drug-seeking behavior
Monitor use; watch for abuse patterns
Monitor for dependence; never abruptly stop

flashcardLinks: Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What does an analgesic drug do?
Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What does an antipyretic drug do?
Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What does an antihistamine do?
Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What is an antimicrobial agent?
Explain the difference between a drug's trade name and generic name

sections.title: Section 1.4: Regulatory Bodies & The FDA Drug Approval Process
learningObjectives: Understand the FDA drug approval timeline and phases
Identify key regulatory bodies and their roles
Appreciate the rigorous testing process before drugs reach patients
keyTakeaways: FDA drug approval takes 10-15 years and costs $2-3 billion
Phase 1: Safety testing in healthy volunteers (20-100 people)
Phase 2: Efficacy testing in patients with target disease (100-500 people)
Phase 3: Large confirmation trials (1,000-5,000 people); if drug passes, moves to NDA
Phase 4: Post-marketing surveillance (ongoing) — MedWatch system catches rare side effects
Black box warnings appear when serious/fatal risks emerge post-approval
Key regulatory bodies: FDA (drug approval), DEA (controlled substances), State Nursing Boards (scope of practice)

agencies.name: FDA (Food & Drug Administration)
agencies.nursingAction: Report serious adverse effects via MedWatch

agencies.name: DEA (Drug Enforcement Administration)
agencies.nursingAction: Follow controlled substance regulations; report diversion

agencies.name: State Pharmacy Boards
agencies.nursingAction: Understand pharmacist scope; collaborate on medication issues

agencies.name: State Nursing Boards
agencies.role: RN licensing, scope of practice definitions
agencies.nursingAction: Know your state's scope; medication administration rights defined here

agencies.name: CDC (Centers for Disease Control)
agencies.role: Disease surveillance, infection control, antibiotic stewardship
agencies.nursingAction: Follow infection control recommendations; use antibiotics judiciously

flashcardLinks: What is the FDA?
Explain the FDA drug approval process
What does the DEA do?

sections.title: Section 1.5: Drug Names & Classification Codes
learningObjectives: Understand the three names every drug has
Prevent medication errors due to drug name confusion
Recognize drug name patterns to predict drug class
keyTakeaways: Generic name = Official chemical designation (same worldwide, lowercase)
Brand name = Manufacturer's proprietary name (capitalized, unique per manufacturer)
Chemical name = Complete chemical description (rarely used clinically, too complex)
Multiple names for same drug = High medication error risk; ALWAYS check generic name
Drug name patterns (-olol, -pril, -statin, etc.) immediately reveal drug class and predictable effects

contentBlocks.title: The Three Names Every Drug Has
Medication Error Risk: The Acetaminophen Overdose
contentBlocks.htmlReady:
Real Scenario: Mrs. Singh takes Tylenol for fever, acetaminophen for pain, and Excedrin Tension for headache — not realizing all three contain acetaminophen. Total: 1,500 mg in one dose (safe) but if repeated 3x daily = 4,500 mg/day (TOXIC; exceeds 4,000 mg/day max).

Result: Acetaminophen toxicity → liver damage → potential liver failure

Prevention: Always check the GENERIC name, not just brand names

flashcardLinks: Explain the difference between a drug's trade name and generic name
What is a chemical drug name?

sections.title: Section 1.6: Pharmacokinetics vs. Pharmacodynamics
learningObjectives: Distinguish between pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics
Understand ADME processes and how they affect drug action
Predict drug effects based on receptor binding mechanisms
Apply PK/PD concepts to clinical decision-making
keyTakeaways: PHARMACOKINETICS = What the body does to the drug (ADME: Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Elimination)
PHARMACODYNAMICS = What the drug does to the body (receptor binding, dose-response, mechanism)
Absorption: Movement from site of administration into bloodstream; affected by route, food, pH, GI motility
Distribution: Movement from bloodstream into tissues; affected by blood flow, lipophilicity, protein binding
Metabolism: Chemical transformation, primarily in liver via cytochrome P450 system
Elimination: Removal of drug from body, primarily via kidneys; affected by renal function
Half-life: Time for drug level to drop 50%; determines dosing frequency
Agonist: Binds receptor and activates it; Antagonist: Binds receptor but doesn't activate it (blocks agonist)
Therapeutic index (TD50/ED50): Ratio of toxic to effective dose; narrow TI = dangerous, wide TI = safe

contentBlocks.title: ADME Process Flow
contentBlocks.description: Step-by-step drug journey through the body

subsections.name: Bioavailability
subsections.definition: Fraction of administered dose reaching systemic circulation

contentBlocks.title: Distribution (D)
factors: Blood flow to tissues (high flow tissues absorb drug faster)
Drug lipophilicity (fat-soluble drugs cross BBB and placenta)
Plasma protein binding (only free drug is active; high binding can cause interactions)
Volume of distribution (Vd) — theoretical measure of drug spread

contentBlocks.title: Metabolism (M)
contentBlocks.description: Drug transformation into metabolites, primarily in liver via cytochrome P450

clinicalConcepts.definition: One drug speeds up metabolism of another
clinicalConcepts.example: Rifampin + oral contraceptive → reduced OCP effectiveness → pregnancy risk

clinicalConcepts.definition: One drug slows down metabolism of another
clinicalConcepts.example: Grapefruit juice + simvastatin → statin level increases 10x → toxicity risk
clinicalConcepts.nursingAction: Advise patients to avoid grapefruit juice

routes.mechanism: Excreted in bile → stored in gallbladder → eliminated in stool

criticalConcept.definition: Time for drug level to drop to 50% of original value
criticalConcept.shortHalfLife: Clears quickly; needs frequent dosing; missing one dose usually OK
criticalConcept.longHalfLife: Clears slowly; dosed once daily or weekly; missing dose matters more

contentBlocks.title: Pharmacodynamics (PD)
contentBlocks.description: What the drug does to the body

receptorTheory.explanation: Drug (key) binds to receptor (lock) → produces effect

types.definition: Binds receptor and produces MAXIMUM effect
Binds receptor and produces SUBMAXIMAL effect (ceiling effect)
Binds receptor but produces NO effect; blocks agonist
types.example: Naloxone (reverses opioid overdose), Metoprolol (blocks beta receptors)

doseResponse.definition: Correlation between amount of drug given and magnitude of effect
doseResponse.ed50: Effective dose producing 50% of max effect
doseResponse.td50: Toxic dose producing toxicity in 50% of people
doseResponse.therapeuticIndex: TD50/ED50 ratio; higher = safer, lower = more dangerous

sections.title: Section 1.7: Drug Interactions & Patient Safety
learningObjectives: Identify pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic drug interactions
Apply drug interaction knowledge to recognize dangerous combinations
Use resources to check for drug interactions
Communicate with prescribers about interaction concerns
keyTakeaways: PHARMACOKINETIC interactions: Changes in absorption, distribution, metabolism, or elimination
PHARMACODYNAMIC interactions: Changes in mechanism of action or combined effects
Additive effects: Drug A + Drug B = combined effect equal to sum of individual effects
Synergistic effects: Combined effect GREATER than additive (e.g., aspirin + ibuprofen GI bleeding)
Antagonistic effects: One drug REDUCES effect of another
Most common dangerous combinations: Warfarin + NSAIDs (bleeding), ACE inhibitor + potassium supplements (hyperkalemia), metformin + IV contrast (lactic acidosis)
Use Medscape Drug Interaction Checker in clinical practice
Manage interactions by: Avoiding combination, changing dose, increasing monitoring, separating timing, or using alternative medication

subtypes.name: Absorption Interactions
subtypes.example: Tetracycline + dairy → reduced absorption (calcium binds drug)

subtypes.name: Distribution Interactions
subtypes.example: Warfarin + NSAIDs → displacement from protein binding → more free warfarin

subtypes.name: Metabolism Interactions
subtypes.example: Rifampin (inducer) + oral contraceptive → increased metabolism → reduced contraceptive effect

subtypes.name: Elimination Interactions
subtypes.example: Probenecid + Penicillin → reduced renal secretion → higher penicillin levels

categories.description: Changes in mechanism of action or combined effects

subtypes.name: Additive Effects
subtypes.example: Alcohol + Benzodiazepine → severe CNS depression (sum of effects)

subtypes.name: Synergistic Effects
subtypes.example: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin → kill bacteria MORE effectively than either alone

subtypes.name: Antagonistic Effects
subtypes.example: Beta-blocker vs. Epinephrine → epinephrine effectiveness reduced

combinations.nursingAction: Check K+ levels; avoid combination if possible
Monitor digoxin level; reduce dose if needed

contentBlocks.title: How to Check for & Manage Interactions

steps.include: What you found, who you contacted, what decision was made

flashcardLinks: What is a drug interaction?
Explain a pharmacokinetic drug interaction using an example
Explain a pharmacodynamic drug interaction using an example
What is a synergistic drug interaction?
What is an additive drug interaction?

sections.title: Section 1.8: Dosage Calculations
learningObjectives: Master three dosage calculation methods: Formula, Ratio-Proportion, Dimensional Analysis
Apply calculations to simple and complex clinical scenarios
Verify calculations and identify dangerous doses
Demonstrate competency in medication dosage calculations
keyTakeaways: Formula Method: Dose = (Desired Dose / Dose on Hand) × Quantity Available
Ratio-Proportion Method: Cross multiply to solve for unknown
Dimensional Analysis Method: Set up fractions so units cancel; if units don't cancel, you made an error
Weight-based dosing common in pediatrics: Dose = mg/kg × patient weight in kg
IV infusion rate: mL/hr = Total Volume / Time in Hours
Always perform clinical check: Does this dose make sense for this patient?
Red flags for dangerous doses: Very large doses, very small decimals, unusual units, inconsistent with patient characteristics
Verification checklist: Right patient, drug, dose, route, time, documentation, reason, response

contentBlocks.title: Method 1: Formula Method
contentBlocks.formula: Dose = (Desired Dose / Dose on Hand) × Quantity Available

components.quantity_available: Number of units (tablets, mL) containing dose on hand

examples.scenario: Amoxicillin 500 mg ordered; on hand 250 mg/tablet
Acetaminophen 160 mg ordered; on hand 80 mg/5 mL
Morphine 10 mg IV ordered; on hand 5 mg/mL

contentBlocks.title: Method 2: Ratio-Proportion Method
contentBlocks.formula: Dose on Hand : Quantity Available :: Desired Dose : X

contentBlocks.title: Method 3: Dimensional Analysis (Preferred)
contentBlocks.description: Set up fractions so units cancel; if units don't cancel, you made an error

examples.calculation: X tablets = 500 mg × (1 tablet / 250 mg) = 2 tablets

examples.scenario: Heparin 1,350 units/hr; on hand 25,000 units/500 mL
examples.calculation: X mL/hr = 1,350 units/hr × (500 mL / 25,000 units) = 27 mL/hr

problems.scenario: Cefixime 8 mg/kg; patient 35 lbs (16 kg); on hand 100 mg/5 mL
stepByStep: Calculate volume: (128 mg / 100 mg) × 5 mL = 6.4 mL ≈ 6.5 mL

problems.scenario: Vancomycin 1000 mg in 250 mL NS over 60 minutes

contentBlocks.title: Red Flags for Dangerous Doses
flags: Very small decimals (0.05 mL when typical is 1-2 mL)
Dose doesn't match patient characteristics (high dose for elderly with renal failure)
Inconsistent with clinical picture (potassium supplement for hyperkalemia patient)
contentBlocks.action: ALWAYS question these; double-check with another nurse; verify with prescriber if uncertain

contentBlocks.title: Before Administering ANY Medication
checkpoints: Right Patient: Check ID against order (2 identifiers)
Right Drug: Check generic name (not just brand name)
Right Dose: Calculate and verify with another nurse if complex
Right Route: Matches order and patient condition
Right Reason: Clinically appropriate for patient's diagnosis
Right Response: Monitor for therapeutic/adverse effects

flashcardLinks: What is the basic formula for calculating drug dose based on body weight?
How do you calculate body surface area (BSA)-based dosing?
What is the formula for calculating IV infusion rates (mL/hr)?
How do you calculate mcg/kg/min dosing?

interactiveElements.prompt: Calculate: Amoxicillin 250 mg ordered; on hand 125 mg/tablet
Calculate: Child weighs 25 lbs; order is 10 mg/kg; on hand 50 mg/5 mL
Calculate: Heparin 12 units/kg/hr for 70 kg patient; on hand 25,000 units/500 mL

sections.title: Section 1.9: Key Terms Glossary
learningObjectives: Master essential pharmacology terminology
Use terminology correctly in clinical communication
Understand precise definitions for accurate professional communication
keyTakeaways: Comprehensive glossary with 100+ terms organized alphabetically (A-W)
Each term includes precise definition relevant to nursing practice
Terms cover pharmacology, pharmacokinetics, pharmacodynamics, drug interactions, and adverse effects

terms.definition: Movement of drug from site of administration into bloodstream
Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Elimination — pharmacokinetic processes
Drug that binds to receptor and activates it, producing an effect
Drug that binds to receptor but doesn't activate it; blocks agonist
Harmful, unexpected drug effect requiring action
Fraction of administered dose reaching systemic circulation in active form
Enzyme system in liver responsible for Phase I drug metabolism; metabolizes ~75% of all drugs
Drug Enforcement Administration; controls regulated substances
Movement of absorbed drug from bloodstream into tissues
Amount of drug given at specific time
Effect of one drug on another's efficacy or safety
Maximum effect achievable by a drug, regardless of dose
Removal of drug from body via kidneys, bile, lungs, etc.
Increase in drug-metabolizing enzyme activity; speeds up metabolism of other drugs
Decrease in drug-metabolizing enzyme activity; slows metabolism of other drugs
Food & Drug Administration; approves and monitors drugs
Drug metabolism in liver before reaching systemic circulation (for oral drugs)
Official chemical designation of drug; same worldwide, usually lowercase
Time for drug concentration to drop to 50% of original value
Primary drug metabolism occurring in the liver
Reason to USE a drug; the disease/condition it treats
Injection directly into vein; fastest route, 100% bioavailability
Chemical breakdown of drug, primarily in liver, to make it water-soluble for excretion
Small difference between therapeutic and toxic dose; requires careful monitoring
Drug administration by mouth; slowest route
Drug binding to receptor with submaximal effect; ceiling effect
What the drug does to the body — mechanism of action, effects
What the body does to the drug — ADME processes
Science of drug effects on living organisms
Reversible attachment of drug molecules to blood proteins; only unbound drug is active
Amount of drug needed to produce given effect; more potent = lower dose needed
Proteins on cell surface or intracellular that drugs bind to
Volume of plasma from which drug is completely removed per unit time
Expected, unintended drug effect; usually manageable
Ratio of toxic dose to effective dose (TD50/ED50); higher = safer
Decreased response to drug after repeated use; requires higher doses
Manufacturer's proprietary brand name for drug; capitalized
Harmful effect from overdose or cumulative effect of drug
Theoretical volume drug distributes into; affects dosing and overdose reversal

flashcardLinks: All terms in glossary have corresponding flashcards for reinforcement

sections.title: Section 1.10: Clinical Story — "The Allergy Decision"
learningObjectives: Apply clinical judgment to medication allergy assessment
Make evidence-based drug selection decisions
Communicate effectively with prescribers
Implement safe medication administration protocols
keyTakeaways: ALWAYS clarify allergy details before assuming incompatibility
Rash-type penicillin allergy (non-anaphylaxis) has <1% cross-reactivity with other beta-lactams
Risk vs. benefit analysis: Sometimes the safest alternative is the best choice
Communication with prescriber is essential for collaborative decision-making
Documentation prevents future providers from repeating work
First-dose monitoring and patient education are critical safety precautions
Nurses are the patient's advocates and last line of defense

contentBlocks.title: Case: Jacob Chen, Pneumonia with Penicillin Allergy

scenario.presentation: Cough, fever, SOB × 4 days; vitals show tachycardia/tachypnea; CXR with left lower lobe infiltrate

decisionPoint1.title: Question 1: Allergy Assessment
decisionPoint1.prompt: What should you do BEFORE administering amoxicillin?

options.text: Give the amoxicillin. The allergy was 25 years ago; it's outdated.
options.feedback: INCORRECT - Dangerous assumption. Penicillin allergy doesn't just go away. Need to assess allergy type first.

options.text: Clarify allergy details with Jacob and communicate with prescriber.
options.feedback: CORRECT - Essential step. Need specifics (was it rash? Anaphylaxis?) before deciding on medication.

options.text: Give Benadryl first to prevent allergic reaction.
options.feedback: INCORRECT - Doesn't address core concern. Benadryl doesn't prevent true anaphylaxis.

decisionPoint1.clinicalReasoning: Allergy clarification is essential for safe decision-making. Rash ≠ anaphylaxis; cross-reactivity risk differs.

allergy_clarification.whatJacobReports: "I took penicillin 25 years ago for strep throat. I developed a rash on arms and back — really itchy. Lips felt tingly, like they were swelling a bit. It went away in 2 hours."
allergy_clarification.riskLevel: Low risk for anaphylaxis (<1% cross-reactivity); but rash/angioedema risk higher

decisionPoint2.title: Question 2: Clinical Decision-Making
decisionPoint2.prompt: Given the allergy clarification, what's the best approach?

options.text: Administer amoxicillin with precautions (epinephrine available, observe 30 min).
options.feedback: ACCEPTABLE but not ideal. Acceptable if prescriber decides to proceed, but doesn't eliminate allergy risk.
CORRECT - Better choice. Eliminates allergy risk; actually preferred for CAP in elderly.

options.text: Refuse to administer. Penicillin allergy is absolute contraindication.
options.feedback: INCORRECT - Overly rigid. Rash-type allergy can be used with precautions; harm if refusing all beta-lactams.

decisionPoint2.clinicalReasoning: Fluoroquinolone is evidence-based alternative for CAP. No allergy risk. Actually preferred for elderly patients.

prescriber_communication.yourMessage: Hi Dr. Smith. I've clarified Jacob's penicillin allergy — it was localized rash with lip swelling 25 years ago, non-anaphylaxis. However, I wanted to suggest levofloxacin 750 mg as an alternative. It's actually ideal for CAP in his age group and avoids cross-reactivity risk. Would that work?
prescriber_communication.result: Dr. Smith agrees; changes to levofloxacin 750 mg × 5 days

decisionPoint3.title: Question 3: Safe Administration & Monitoring
decisionPoint3.prompt: How do you safely administer the new medication?

options.feedback: INCOMPLETE - While true, first dose of ANY antibiotic deserves monitoring for rare adverse events.

options.text: Observe 30 min post-dose, educate patient, have epinephrine available.
options.feedback: CORRECT - Comprehensive safety protocol: monitoring, education, preparedness.

options.text: Skip monitoring; fluoroquinolones are well-studied, low allergy risk.
options.feedback: INCOMPLETE - While true, standard of care includes first-dose monitoring.

decisionPoint3.clinicalReasoning: Standard first-dose monitoring captures rare adverse events. Patient education ensures he knows what to report.

steps: Administer levofloxacin 750 mg PO with water
Observe × 30 minutes; check vitals at 15 and 30 min
Watch for: rash, tingling, breathing difficulty, dizziness
Educate: Complete full course; avoid sun; no food interactions
Document: Drug given, observation completed, patient educated, no adverse reaction

contentBlocks.outcome: Jacob tolerates levofloxacin well. Fever resolves by day 3; pneumonia resolved by discharge. Follow-up confirms full recovery.

lessons: Always clarify allergies; don't assume old records are complete or accurate
Risk vs. benefit analysis is essential for clinical judgment
Communication with prescriber enables collaborative, evidence-based decisions
Documentation prevents future errors and saves time
Pharmacology knowledge is patient safety in action

interactiveElements.prompt: What would you do differently if Jacob reported anaphylaxis instead of rash?
Role-play contacting the prescriber about the allergy concern

sections.title: Section 1.11: Review Questions & Assessment
learningObjectives: Assess understanding of chapter content
Apply knowledge to clinical decision-making
Identify areas needing further study
keyTakeaways: 8 comprehensive review questions covering major chapter topics
Multiple choice questions test foundational knowledge
Short answer questions assess deeper understanding
Case-based question integrates multiple concepts into realistic scenario

questions.question: A nursing student asks you to explain the difference between pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics. Which statement best describes their relationship?

options.A: Pharmacokinetics is what the drug does to the body, and pharmacodynamics is what the body does to the drug
options.B: Pharmacokinetics is what the body does to the drug, and pharmacodynamics is what the drug does to the body
options.C: Pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics are the same concept, just different names
options.D: Pharmacodynamics involves drug metabolism only; pharmacokinetics involves all other processes

questions.rationale: PK = what body does to drug (ADME); PD = what drug does to body (mechanism of action, receptor binding)

questions.question: A patient has a documented 'penicillin allergy — rash, 15 years ago.' The prescriber orders amoxicillin for UTI. What is your best initial action?

options.A: Administer immediately; allergy was long ago and unlikely to recur
options.B: Refuse to administer; penicillin allergy is absolute contraindication
options.C: Clarify allergy details with patient and communicate with prescriber before administering
options.D: Give Benadryl prophylactically to prevent reaction

questions.rationale: Allergy clarification is essential. Rash ≠ anaphylaxis. Inform prescriber; make collaborative decision.

questions.question: A patient on digoxin (narrow TI) is prescribed verapamil for atrial fib. What is your primary concern?

options.A: No interaction; both lower heart rate, which is desired
options.B: Verapamil inhibits digoxin metabolism → digoxin level increases → toxicity risk
options.C: Digoxin is metabolized too quickly with verapamil
options.D: No interaction; digoxin is older drug without modern interactions

questions.rationale: Verapamil is CYP3A4 inhibitor; decreases digoxin elimination → toxicity (arrhythmias, nausea)

questions.question: Patient weighs 60 lbs. Order is amoxicillin 25 mg/kg. Calculate total dose.
Based on drug name 'clopidogrel,' what can you infer?

options.A: It's a beta-blocker used to lower heart rate/BP
options.B: It's an antiplatelet agent (-grel suffix) likely used to prevent blood clots
options.C: It's an antibiotic due to the -dol suffix

questions.rationale: Thienopyridine antiplatelet agents end in -grel; immediately reveals drug class and expected effects

questions.question: Explain the clinical significance of 'narrow therapeutic index' and give one specific drug example. How would you adjust your nursing care?
expectedAnswerElements: Definition: Small difference between effective and toxic dose
Nursing adjustments: Monitor drug levels, watch for toxicity signs, assess for interactions, monitor renal function, check electrolytes, educate patient

questions.question: A 78-year-old with mild kidney disease (GFR 45) is prescribed metformin for diabetes. An IV contrast procedure is ordered. Identify concerns and outline nursing actions.
expectedAnswerElements: Concern 1: Metformin + IV contrast = contrast-induced AKI → metformin accumulation → lactic acidosis
Concern 2: Baseline renal impairment increases risk
Nursing actions: Alert prescriber; implement metformin-contrast protocol; hold metformin 24-48h before/after contrast; recheck renal function; monitor for lactic acidosis signs

questions.question: Mr. Park, 82-year-old with CHF exacerbation on: furosemide, lisinopril, metoprolol, digoxin, spironolactone. New order: ibuprofen 600 mg TID for arthritis. Identify ALL interactions, prioritize by severity, describe nursing response.
expectedAnswerElements: MAJOR: Triple whammy (NSAID + ACE inhibitor + diuretic) → AKI risk
MAJOR: NSAID + spironolactone + lisinopril → hyperkalemia risk
MAJOR: NSAID reduces digoxin elimination → toxicity risk

Nursing response: DO NOT ADMINISTER; contact prescriber; propose acetaminophen or topical NSAID alternative; if prescriber insists, implement strict monitoring protocol with baseline labs, daily clinical checks, repeat labs at day 3-7

titles: Role of Nurses in Medication Safety

flashcards.question: Define pharmacology.
flashcards.answer: Pharmacology is the science that deals with the composition, properties, reactions, and effects of drugs, including their origin, preparation, uses, and effects on living organisms.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics?
flashcards.answer: Pharmacokinetics is what the body does to the drug (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion). Pharmacodynamics is what the drug does to the body (mechanism of action and effects).

flashcards.question: Define efficacy in the context of drug effects.
flashcards.answer: Efficacy is the maximum therapeutic effect a drug can produce, regardless of dose. It reflects the drug's ability to produce the desired response.

flashcards.question: What is potency?
flashcards.answer: Potency refers to the amount of drug needed to produce a given effect. A more potent drug requires a lower dose to achieve the same effect as a less potent drug.

flashcards.question: Define drug selectivity (or selective toxicity).
flashcards.answer: Drug selectivity is the ability of a drug to affect target cells/tissues while minimizing effects on non-target cells. Greater selectivity means fewer side effects.

flashcards.question: What is a therapeutic drug action?
flashcards.answer: A therapeutic drug action is the desired, beneficial effect of a drug that produces the intended clinical outcome (e.g., lowering blood pressure with an antihypertensive).

flashcards.question: Fill in the blank: A _____ is an undesired, potentially harmful drug effect that occurs at therapeutic doses.
flashcards.answer: adverse effect (or adverse drug reaction, side effect)

flashcards.question: Explain the difference between a drug's trade name and generic name.
flashcards.answer: A generic name (nonproprietary name) is the official chemical or pharmacological name assigned by regulatory bodies (e.g., ibuprofen). A trade name (brand name) is the proprietary name given by the pharmaceutical manufacturer (e.g., Advil®).

flashcards.question: What is a chemical drug name?
flashcards.answer: A chemical drug name is the precise description of the drug's chemical composition and molecular structure (e.g., acetyl salicylic acid for aspirin). It is used primarily in research and development.

flashcards.question: Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What does an analgesic drug do?
flashcards.answer: An analgesic relieves pain without causing unconsciousness. Examples include aspirin, ibuprofen, and morphine.

flashcards.question: Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What does an antipyretic drug do?
flashcards.answer: An antipyretic reduces fever. Common examples include acetaminophen and ibuprofen.

flashcards.question: Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What does an antihistamine do?
flashcards.answer: An antihistamine blocks histamine receptors, preventing or reducing histamine effects such as allergic reactions, itching, and swelling. Examples include diphenhydramine and loratadine.

flashcards.question: Classify drugs by therapeutic action: What is an antimicrobial agent?
flashcards.answer: An antimicrobial agent inhibits or kills microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites). Examples include antibiotics, antivirals, antifungals, and antiparasitics.

flashcards.question: What is drug absorption?
flashcards.answer: Drug absorption is the movement of a drug from its site of administration into the bloodstream. It depends on the route of administration, drug properties, and physiological factors.

flashcards.question: What is bioavailability?
flashcards.answer: Bioavailability is the percentage of administered drug that reaches systemic circulation in its active form. Intravenous administration has 100% bioavailability; other routes have lower bioavailability.

flashcards.question: Which route of administration has the fastest onset of action?
flashcards.answer: Intravenous (IV) administration has the fastest onset because the drug is injected directly into the bloodstream, bypassing the absorption phase.

flashcards.question: Rank these routes by speed of absorption: oral, sublingual, intramuscular, intravenous.
flashcards.answer: Fastest to slowest: intravenous > intramuscular > sublingual > oral. IV has immediate effect; oral is slowest due to GI absorption variability.

flashcards.question: What factors affect oral drug absorption?
flashcards.answer: Factors include: pH of GI tract, gastric motility, food presence, drug solubility, first-pass metabolism, and the condition of GI mucosa (e.g., inflammation, ulcers).

flashcards.question: What is first-pass metabolism (first-pass effect)?
flashcards.answer: First-pass metabolism occurs when an orally administered drug is absorbed from the GI tract and transported directly to the liver via the hepatic portal vein, where it may be significantly metabolized before reaching systemic circulation. This reduces drug bioavailability.

flashcards.question: How does food affect oral drug absorption?
flashcards.answer: Food can increase, decrease, or have no effect on absorption depending on the drug. Food increases absorption of fat-soluble drugs, decreases absorption of some antibiotics, and delays absorption by slowing gastric emptying.

flashcards.question: Define drug distribution.
flashcards.answer: Drug distribution is the movement of a drug from the blood to various body tissues and organs. It depends on blood flow to tissues, drug lipid solubility, plasma protein binding, and blood-brain barrier permeability.

flashcards.question: What is plasma protein binding, and why is it clinically important?
flashcards.answer: Plasma protein binding is the reversible attachment of drug molecules to blood proteins (mainly albumin). Only unbound drug is pharmacologically active. High protein binding can lead to drug interactions when two drugs compete for binding sites.

flashcards.question: What is the blood-brain barrier (BBB), and which drugs can cross it?
flashcards.answer: The blood-brain barrier is a selective barrier formed by tight junctions in cerebral capillaries that restricts passage of polar/hydrophilic substances. Only lipophilic (fat-soluble) drugs and small polar molecules can cross it. This is why many drugs don't treat CNS conditions.

flashcards.question: Define drug metabolism (biotransformation).
flashcards.answer: Drug metabolism is the chemical modification of a drug by the body, primarily in the liver, to make it more water-soluble for excretion. Metabolism can produce active metabolites (that have effects) or inactive metabolites.

flashcards.question: What are the three phases of drug metabolism?
flashcards.answer: Phase I (oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis) - primarily via cytochrome P450 enzymes; Phase II (conjugation) - attachment of water-soluble molecules; Phase III (transport) - active movement of drug/metabolites out of cells for elimination.

flashcards.question: What is the cytochrome P450 system?
flashcards.answer: The cytochrome P450 (CYP450) system is a group of enzymes in the liver and other tissues that catalyze Phase I metabolism. It's responsible for metabolizing approximately 75% of all drugs. Genetic variations affect metabolism rates (poor vs. rapid metabolizers).

flashcards.question: What is a drug inducer, and give an example.
flashcards.answer: A drug inducer is a substance that increases the activity of metabolic enzymes (especially CYP450), leading to faster drug metabolism and potentially lower drug levels. Example: rifampin increases metabolism of oral contraceptives, reducing their effectiveness.

flashcards.question: What is a drug inhibitor, and give an example.
flashcards.answer: A drug inhibitor is a substance that decreases the activity of metabolic enzymes, leading to slower drug metabolism and potentially higher drug levels. Example: ketoconazole inhibits CYP450, increasing levels of drugs metabolized by these enzymes.

flashcards.question: What is hepatic metabolism, and which organs can metabolize drugs?
flashcards.answer: Hepatic metabolism is the primary metabolism of drugs in the liver. However, drugs can also be metabolized in the kidneys, lungs, GI tract, and other tissues. The liver is responsible for ~90% of drug metabolism.

flashcards.question: Define drug excretion.
flashcards.answer: Drug excretion is the elimination of a drug or its metabolites from the body. The kidneys are the primary route (via filtration, secretion, reabsorption), but excretion also occurs via bile, lungs, skin, and feces.

flashcards.question: What is renal clearance, and why is it important?
flashcards.answer: Renal clearance is the volume of plasma from which a drug is completely removed per unit time. It's important because impaired kidney function reduces drug clearance, leading to drug accumulation and toxicity risk. Dosing adjustments are needed for patients with renal impairment.

flashcards.question: How does renal disease affect drug excretion?
flashcards.answer: Renal disease decreases glomerular filtration rate (GFR), reducing the filtration of drugs and metabolites. This causes drug accumulation in the body, increasing the risk of toxicity. Dose adjustments based on GFR are necessary.

flashcards.question: Fill in the blank: The half-life of a drug is the time required for the plasma concentration to decrease to _____ of its original value.
flashcards.answer: 50% (or one-half)

flashcards.question: Why is drug half-life clinically important for nursing practice?
flashcards.answer: Half-life determines dosing intervals. Drugs with short half-lives require more frequent dosing; those with long half-lives require less frequent dosing. It also helps predict time to reach steady state and time for drug elimination from the body.

flashcards.question: Define steady state in pharmacokinetics.
flashcards.answer: Steady state is when the amount of drug administered equals the amount eliminated per unit time, resulting in constant plasma drug concentrations. It's typically reached after 4-5 half-lives and is important for maintaining therapeutic effects.

flashcards.question: What is pharmacodynamics?
flashcards.answer: Pharmacodynamics is the study of what the drug does to the body—its mechanisms of action, effects on body systems, and the relationship between drug concentration and therapeutic/adverse effects.

flashcards.question: Define drug receptor and explain the receptor theory of drug action.
flashcards.answer: A drug receptor is a specific cellular protein (on cell membrane or intracellular) with which a drug binds. The receptor theory states that drugs produce effects by binding to specific receptors, causing conformational changes that initiate a cellular response.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between agonists and antagonists?
flashcards.answer: An agonist binds to a receptor and produces a pharmacological effect (activates the receptor). An antagonist binds to a receptor but does not activate it; instead, it blocks agonists from binding and prevents receptor activation.

flashcards.question: Define partial agonist.
flashcards.answer: A partial agonist binds to a receptor and produces a submaximal response—it produces effects but with lower efficacy than a full agonist. It can act as an agonist or antagonist depending on the clinical context.

flashcards.question: What is the dose-response relationship?
flashcards.answer: The dose-response relationship is the correlation between the amount of drug administered (dose) and the magnitude of the resulting effect (response). As dose increases, response typically increases until maximum efficacy is reached.

flashcards.question: What is the therapeutic index (TI), and why does it matter?
flashcards.answer: The therapeutic index is the ratio of the toxic dose to the effective dose (TD50/ED50). A higher TI indicates a wider margin of safety between therapeutic and toxic doses. A lower TI means the drug has a narrower safety margin and requires careful dose monitoring.

flashcards.question: Give three examples of drugs with a narrow therapeutic index.
flashcards.answer: Examples include: digoxin (cardiac glycoside), warfarin (anticoagulant), and lithium (mood stabilizer). These drugs require close monitoring and dose adjustment.

flashcards.question: What is the basic formula for calculating drug dose based on body weight?
flashcards.answer: Dose = Prescribed dose per kg (or lb) × Patient's weight. Example: If 5 mg/kg is prescribed and patient weighs 70 kg, dose = 5 mg/kg × 70 kg = 350 mg.

flashcards.question: How do you calculate body surface area (BSA)-based dosing?
flashcards.answer: BSA is typically calculated using height and weight (in specialized nomograms or formulas). Dose = Prescribed dose per m² × Patient's BSA in m². BSA-based dosing is common for chemotherapy agents.

flashcards.question: What is the formula for calculating IV infusion rates (mL/hr)?
flashcards.answer: Infusion rate (mL/hr) = Total volume (mL) ÷ Infusion time (hours). For drops/min: Drops/min = (Total volume × Drop factor) ÷ Time in minutes. Drop factor varies by tubing (typically 10, 15, or 20 drops/mL).

flashcards.question: How do you calculate mcg/kg/min dosing?
flashcards.answer: Dose (mcg/kg/min) × Weight (kg) × 60 min/hr ÷ Concentration (mcg/mL) = Infusion rate (mL/hr). This is commonly used for critical care medications like vasopressors and inotropes.

flashcards.question: What is a drug interaction?
flashcards.answer: A drug interaction occurs when one drug affects the action of another drug, leading to increased, decreased, or altered effects. Interactions can be pharmacokinetic (affecting absorption, distribution, metabolism, or excretion) or pharmacodynamic (affecting mechanisms of action).

flashcards.question: Explain a pharmacokinetic drug interaction using an example.
flashcards.answer: A pharmacokinetic interaction affects ADME processes. Example: St. John's Wort induces CYP450 enzymes, increasing metabolism of oral contraceptives, reducing contraceptive effectiveness.

flashcards.question: Explain a pharmacodynamic drug interaction using an example.
flashcards.answer: A pharmacodynamic interaction occurs at the receptor level or through mechanisms of action. Example: Combining two CNS depressants (alcohol + benzodiazepine) causes additive effects, resulting in excessive sedation and respiratory depression.

flashcards.question: What is a synergistic drug interaction?
flashcards.answer: A synergistic interaction is when two drugs together produce an effect greater than the sum of their individual effects. Example: Aspirin + ibuprofen together cause greater gastrointestinal irritation than either alone.

flashcards.question: What is an additive drug interaction?
flashcards.answer: An additive interaction is when two drugs with similar effects combine to produce a total effect equal to the sum of their individual effects. Example: Two antihypertensive agents together lower blood pressure more than either alone, by an additive amount.

flashcards.question: Define an adverse drug reaction (ADR).
flashcards.answer: An adverse drug reaction is any unwanted or harmful effect produced by a drug at therapeutic doses. ADRs range from mild (nausea) to severe (anaphylaxis, organ failure) and are a major cause of morbidity and mortality.

flashcards.question: Classify adverse drug reactions: What is a Type A reaction?
flashcards.answer: A Type A (Augmented) reaction is an exaggeration of a drug's known pharmacological effect due to overdose or increased sensitivity. It is dose-dependent, predictable, and reversible upon dose reduction. Example: Excessive bleeding with warfarin.

flashcards.question: Classify adverse drug reactions: What is a Type B reaction?
flashcards.answer: A Type B (Bizarre) reaction is unexpected, idiosyncratic, and unrelated to the drug's known pharmacological action. It is not dose-dependent, unpredictable, and potentially serious. Example: Penicillin allergy causing anaphylaxis.

flashcards.question: What is a drug allergy, and how does it differ from a side effect?
flashcards.answer: A drug allergy is an immunologic reaction (involving antibodies/T-cells) to a drug, ranging from mild rash to anaphylaxis. It is an ADR but not typical pharmacology. A side effect is an expected, predictable adverse effect from a drug's known action (e.g., drowsiness from antihistamine).

flashcards.question: What is anaphylaxis, and list three signs/symptoms.
flashcards.answer: Anaphylaxis is a severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that occurs within minutes of exposure. Signs/symptoms include: rapid onset urticaria/pruritus, angioedema, bronchospasm/dyspnea, hypotension, tachycardia, and loss of consciousness.

flashcards.question: What is the nursing management for anaphylaxis due to a drug?
flashcards.answer: Immediate management: Stop the drug, position patient supine, elevate legs, establish IV access, administer epinephrine IM immediately, give oxygen, monitor vitals, have emergency equipment ready, and notify physician. Document reaction details and update allergy list.

flashcards.question: Define tolerance in the context of pharmacology.
flashcards.answer: Drug tolerance is a decreased response to a drug after repeated use, requiring higher doses to achieve the same effect. It occurs through multiple mechanisms: receptor downregulation, enzyme induction, and physiological adaptation.

flashcards.question: What is cross-tolerance?
flashcards.answer: Cross-tolerance is the development of tolerance to a drug that extends to other drugs of the same class or with similar mechanisms. Example: A patient tolerant to morphine will also show tolerance to codeine.

flashcards.question: Define drug dependence and distinguish physical from psychological dependence.
flashcards.answer: Drug dependence is the need to continue taking a drug to avoid withdrawal symptoms or maintain function. Physical dependence: withdrawal produces physiological symptoms (tremors, sweating). Psychological dependence: withdrawal produces psychological symptoms (anxiety, depression) without physical withdrawal.

quiz.question: Define pharmacology and its relationship to nursing practice.
options: The study of drugs and their effects on living organisms
quiz.explanation: Pharmacology is the science dealing with drug composition, properties, and effects on living organisms. It's essential to nursing for safe medication administration.

quiz.question: What is the primary difference between pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics?
options: Pharmacokinetics = what the body does to the drug; Pharmacodynamics = what the drug does to the body
Pharmacokinetics = drug safety; Pharmacodynamics = drug effectiveness
Pharmacokinetics = IV only; Pharmacodynamics = oral only
There is no difference between them
quiz.explanation: Pharmacokinetics describes absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion. Pharmacodynamics describes the drug's mechanism of action and effects.

quiz.question: A nurse notes a medication order that seems high for the patient's age and weight. What should she do?
options: Wait to see if there's a correction
Contact the prescriber to verify the order
Reduce the dose based on her judgment
quiz.explanation: Patient safety is paramount. Any questionable orders should be verified with the prescriber before administration.

quiz.question: Which route of administration has the fastest onset of action?
quiz.explanation: IV administration delivers the drug directly into systemic circulation, resulting in immediate onset compared to other routes.

quiz.question: What is first-pass metabolism?
options: The initial absorption of a drug in the stomach
When oral drugs are metabolized by the liver before reaching systemic circulation
The distribution of drugs to peripheral tissues
The elimination of drugs through the kidneys
quiz.explanation: First-pass metabolism reduces bioavailability of orally administered drugs because they pass through the liver first.

quiz.question: A patient is taking warfarin and NSAIDs together. What is the primary concern?
quiz.explanation: Both warfarin and NSAIDs increase bleeding risk. Combined use is a dangerous drug interaction that should be avoided.

quiz.question: What is therapeutic index?
options: The ratio of toxic dose to therapeutic dose
The number of side effects a drug causes
The speed at which a drug works
quiz.explanation: Therapeutic index (TI) indicates drug safety. A high TI means a wider margin between effective and toxic doses.

quiz.question: Calculate: A patient needs 500 mg of amoxicillin. You have 250 mg tablets on hand. How many tablets do you give?
quiz.explanation: (500 mg ÷ 250 mg) × 1 tablet = 2 tablets. Using the formula method: Dose = (Desired ÷ On Hand) × Quantity.

keyTermsAndDefinitions.definition: The science that deals with the composition, properties, reactions, and effects of drugs.
The study of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME).
The study of drug mechanisms of action and effects on the body.
The percentage of administered drug that reaches systemic circulation in its active form.
The hepatic metabolism of oral drugs before they reach systemic circulation.
The modification of one drug's effect by another drug, food, or substance.
The ratio of toxic dose to therapeutic dose; indicates drug safety margin.
The maximum therapeutic effect a drug can produce, regardless of dose.
The amount of drug needed to produce a given effect; a measure of drug strength.
An undesired and potentially harmful effect that occurs at therapeutic doses.
